title: Provider Supply, Utilization, and Infant Health: Evidence from a Physician Distribution Policy

construct_vars: df <- df %>% dplyr::left_join(gvar_tbl, by='codigo') %>% dplyr::mutate(gvar_CS = ifelse(!is.na(first_policy), as.numeric(first_policy), 0))

notes: EVENT STUDY REVIEWED. All 5 estimators converge beautifully. Growing effect 0→0.12. Near-perfect TWFE/CS agreement. Carrillo, Feres (2019). Table 2 Col 5. Stata: areg physician_cpta POLICY i.UF#c.date c.(bolsafamilia spending_education spending_sanitation_health)#c.date c.(medico_preMPP)#c.date c.(GDP_cpta lnPopulation2010 ...)#c.date i.date, a(codigo) cluster(codigo). ALL controls enter as var*date interactions (NOT levels). State-specific linear trends: i.UF#c.date = UF[date] in feols. 5565 municipalities, bimonthly periods. Target: 0.116 (SE 0.009). | Outcome selection: Rule (ii) largest N. Main DiD result is physician supply (Table 4-5, N~18k). Infant health outcomes (Table 6) are supplementary/null. Audit confirmed. | LESSON 7 CASE STUDY (2026-04-19): clean matched-protocol example. Spec A (TWFE+18ctrls vs CS+18ctrls, status=OK, no Pattern 42 degeneracy): β=0.116 vs 0.097 → matched gap 16.4%. Spec B (unconditional both): 0.128 vs 0.114 → gap 11%. Spec C (legacy asymmetric, TWFE+ctrls vs CS no-ctrls): 0.116 vs 0.114 → apparent concordance 1.3% — MISLEADING. The asymmetric protocol (C) hides the genuine estimator divergence (A). This is the textbook example of why Lesson 7 insists on protocol matching.
